pr.title: name and clean ftNess files and functions
pr.author: VetriTheRetri

summary: PR #397 named and cleaned the ftNess code by assigning names to all ftNess functions, replacing hardcoded values with enums/macros where possible, naming several shared helper functions, adding fighter attribute definitions for Reflect/Absorb/Shield, and expanding/renaming item ID definitions from id.h to itkind.h. It also noted item2.c as complete but not enabled in obj_files.mk because it did not compile correctly.
what_changed: Named all ftNess functions according to the PR body.
Updated ftNess files to use enums and macros instead of hardcoded values wherever possible.
Added Reflect, Absorb, and Shield attribute definitions to fighter.h.
Expanded id.h and renamed it to itkind.h.
Marked item2.c as complete, but left it unused in obj_files.mk because of incorrect compilation.
smart_moves: Converted hardcoded ftNess values to enums/macros, improving readability and making future matching work less dependent on unexplained literals.
Named shared helper functions around animation frame checks, collision reflect/absorb hit creation, and ground/ledge checking, making callsites easier to understand across systems.
Kept item2.c out of obj_files.mk despite considering it complete, avoiding a build integration step known to compile incorrectly.
Moved item kind naming toward a more specific header name, itkind.h, which better communicates the header's purpose than a generic id.h name.
decomp_lessons: When cleaning fighter-specific files, replace magic constants with project-wide enums and macros where equivalent semantic definitions exist.
Function naming can be valuable even without new matching progress if it clarifies repeated helper behavior used by fighter, collision, or environment systems.
Do not wire a supposedly complete source file into obj_files.mk until its compilation behavior is verified; document the reason when leaving it unused.
Header renames should improve semantic specificity, as with id.h becoming itkind.h for item kind definitions.
Attribute definitions such as Reflect, Absorb, and Shield belong in shared fighter headers when they are used as fighter attributes rather than local ftNess-only constants.
naming_conventions: ftNess functions use the fighter prefix ftNess.
Animation helper naming used ftAnim_IsFramesRemaining for func_8006F238.
Fighter collision helper naming used ftColl_CreateReflectHit for func_8007B23C.
Fighter collision helper naming used ftColl_CreateAbsorbHit for func_8007B2C4.
Environment collision helper naming used EnvColl_CheckGroundAndLedge for func_800822A4.
Item kind definitions were moved toward the more explicit header name itkind.h.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: The PR context does not include a diff or assembly details, so no specific instruction scheduling, register allocation, or matching tactics can be confirmed.
The explicit tactic evidenced by the PR body is semantic cleanup: use enums/macros instead of hardcoded values to preserve meaning while maintaining source equivalence.
The integration tactic for item2.c was conservative: keep a completed but incorrectly compiling file out of obj_files.mk.
review_feedback: No issue comments, review comments, or reviews were present in the provided PR context.
No reviewer-requested changes or naming corrections can be inferred from the available evidence.
searchable_terms: name and clean ftNess files and functions
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that enabled item2.c in obj_files.mk or fixed its incorrect compilation.
Search for additional uses or refinements of ftColl_CreateReflectHit and ftColl_CreateAbsorbHit.
Check later changes to itkind.h for item ID enum naming conventions.
Look for subsequent ftNess cleanup or matching PRs that build on these function names.
Verify whether Reflect, Absorb, and Shield attributes in fighter.h were reused by other fighters.
